state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
product_description: Cloud data warehouse with ETL pipeline builder, SQL query engine, data catalog, lineage tracking, access control, and BI tool integration.

plan.description: Define product requirements document, roadmap, and success metrics for the cloud data warehouse platform covering ETL, SQL engine, catalog, lineage, RBAC, and BI integration.
acceptance_criteria: PRD documents all six core feature areas with scope boundaries
Roadmap has at least three release milestones with defined scope per milestone
Success metrics defined: query latency p95, pipeline success rate, catalog coverage percentage
Prioritization matrix ranks features by user value and implementation complexity

plan.description: Capture business requirements, user stories, and process flows for all personas: data engineers (ETL), data analysts (SQL/BI), data stewards (catalog/lineage), and admins (RBAC).
acceptance_criteria: Minimum 30 user stories covering all six feature areas
Process flow diagrams for ETL authoring, query execution, catalog search, and access request workflows
ROI analysis quantifies cost savings vs Snowflake/BigQuery for target customer segment
Acceptance criteria defined per user story
Integration points with existing systems mapped
Migration plan drafted

plan.description: Draft terms of service, privacy policy, data processing agreements (DPA), and open-source license inventory for all third-party dependencies.
acceptance_criteria: ToS covers data ownership, SLA commitments, and acceptable use
Privacy policy addresses data retention, deletion rights, and cross-border transfers
DPA template is GDPR Article 28 compliant
OSS license audit flags any GPL/AGPL conflicts with commercial distribution

plan.description: Map ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II controls to product architecture. Produce a control inventory, risk register, and traceability matrix linking each control to implementation evidence.
acceptance_criteria: All Annex A ISO 27001 controls assessed as in-scope, out-of-scope, or N/A with justification
SOC 2 TSC criteria mapped to specific implementation tasks
Risk register has likelihood/impact ratings and mitigation owners for top 20 risks
Evidence collection plan specifies artifact type, owner, and collection cadence per control

plan.description: Produce threat model (STRIDE), security architecture document, encryption-at-rest and in-transit design, and penetration test plan covering multi-tenant data isolation.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE threat model identifies threats for all six trust boundaries
Security architecture specifies AES-256 at-rest, TLS 1.3 in-transit, and key rotation strategy
Multi-tenant isolation design prevents cross-tenant data access at storage and query layers
Pen test plan covers OWASP Top 10 plus data warehouse-specific attack vectors (SQL injection, metadata poisoning)

plan.description: Conduct user research and produce wireframes for: ETL pipeline canvas, SQL query editor, data catalog browser, lineage graph viewer, RBAC admin panel, and BI connector setup.

Pipeline canvas supports drag-and-drop node placement with connector arrows
Lineage graph is interactive (zoom, pan, click-to-expand)
Accessibility audit confirms WCAG 2.1 AA compliance on all key flows

plan.description: Design OpenAPI 3.1 spec for all platform APIs: warehouse management, ETL pipeline CRUD and execution, SQL query submission and results, catalog search and metadata, lineage graph queries, RBAC policies, and BI connector registration.

plan.description: Design and implement database schemas for: warehouse metadata store, ETL pipeline definitions and run history, data catalog assets and tags, lineage graph (adjacency), RBAC policies, and query history.

plan.description: Build the ETL pipeline engine: visual DAG executor supporting sources (S3, Postgres, MySQL, Kafka, REST API), transformations (filter, map, join, aggregate, custom Python/SQL), and sinks (warehouse tables, S3, webhooks). Include scheduling (cron + event-driven) and retry/backoff logic.

payload.execution_model: Apache Airflow-compatible DAG or custom async Python executor

acceptance_criteria: Pipeline with 5 nodes (source → filter → transform → join → sink) executes end-to-end

plan.description: Build the SQL query engine: distributed query planner and executor on top of Apache Iceberg tables (using Trino or DuckDB for query processing), with query result caching, execution plan explain, and resource governance (per-user query quotas).

acceptance_criteria: SELECT query on 1B row Iceberg table returns first page of results within 5 seconds
Query result cache achieves cache-hit within 100ms for repeated identical queries
EXPLAIN ANALYZE returns execution plan with per-stage row counts and timing

Concurrent queries from different tenants do not share result cache entries
WebSocket streaming delivers rows incrementally without buffering full result set

plan.description: Produce user guides, onboarding documentation, and training materials for all four personas: data engineers (ETL pipeline authoring), data analysts (SQL editor and BI integration), data stewards (catalog curation), and admins (RBAC and monitoring).

Role-based training paths defined

plan.description: Write architecture documentation: system design document, data flow diagrams, ADRs (Architecture Decision Records) for key choices (Iceberg over Delta Lake, Trino over Spark, OPA for authz), deployment topology diagram, and developer onboarding guide.

deliverables: System design document with component diagram
Data flow diagrams for ETL, query, catalog, and lineage flows
ADRs: table format selection, query engine selection, auth engine selection, graph store selection
Deployment topology diagram (Kubernetes, managed services, CDN)
Developer onboarding guide (local setup in under 30 minutes)
payload.format: Markdown + Mermaid diagrams committed to repo

acceptance_criteria: System design document reviewed and approved by tech lead
All ADRs include context, decision, consequences, and alternatives considered
Developer onboarding guide tested by a new engineer who achieves local setup within 30 minutes
Mermaid diagrams render correctly in GitHub and documentation site
Data flow diagrams cover happy path and error/retry flows
Documentation kept in sync with code via CI check on API schema changes

flaws: Step 12 specifies 'Python UDF sandbox prevents filesystem and network access' with zero implementation detail. No sandboxing technology is named (gVisor, Firecracker, seccomp profiles, nsjail). This is the highest-severity RCE vector in the entire plan and is treated as a one-line acceptance criterion. Production UDF sandboxing is a multi-sprint effort in itself.

Step 15 column-level lineage from ANTLR SQL parsing is massively underscoped. The acceptance criterion of '50 representative SQL patterns including JOINs, CTEs, and window functions' is a toy benchmark. Commercial lineage tools (Alation, Atlan, OpenMetadata) with years of engineering still fail on dynamic SQL, stored procedures, MERGE statements, lateral joins, and dialect variations. This will be the #1 source of post-launch bugs.

Step 16 builds JDBC/ODBC drivers and native connectors for Tableau, Power BI, Looker, Metabase, and Superset as a single plan step. Each of these is a substantial engineering project: Tableau has its own connector SDK and certification process, Power BI DirectQuery requires specific query folding semantics, Looker LookML export requires understanding the LookML schema model. This is 6-12 months of work presented as one step.

Step 13 performance SLA ('SELECT query on 1B row Iceberg table returns first page within 5 seconds') is an unqualified promise. It doesn't specify cluster size, query shape, partition pruning, whether data is cached, cold vs warm path, or network topology. This will be used in sales materials and will fail for non-trivial queries.

Multi-tenant isolation is mentioned throughout but never architecturally resolved. How does Trino enforce tenant isolation at query runtime? Separate catalogs per tenant? Shared catalog with row-filter policies? What happens to the query result cache in Redis — are keys namespaced per tenant? Elasticsearch: index-per-tenant or document-level security? These gaps are where cross-tenant leakage actually happens.

Step 8 specifies 7+ data stores: PostgreSQL, Elasticsearch, Neo4j/Neptune, TimescaleDB, Redis, S3, and the query engine's own storage. No single step addresses the operational burden of running all of these. The Docker Compose in Step 9 will be enormous and slow. The on-call burden is proportional to the number of distinct stores.

The Iceberg catalog backend is never decided. The plan uses Iceberg throughout (Steps 10, 13, 14) but never selects a metastore: AWS Glue, Hive Metastore, Iceberg REST Catalog, or Project Nessie. This choice affects the query engine, ETL engine, and catalog service at their foundation. It's a day-one architectural decision that's missing.

Step 9 specifies HashiCorp Vault as a hard startup dependency for all services with no Vault HA topology, no agent sidecar pattern, and no documented fallback. If Vault is unavailable at startup, every service fails to boot. This creates a single point of failure that invalidates the 99.9% availability SLO from Step 25.

Step 26 load test profile (100 concurrent query users, 10 ETL pipelines, 30 minutes) is inadequate for a product positioning against Snowflake and BigQuery. It proves nothing about production headroom. No mention of data volume at test time, no ramp-up simulation, no spike scenarios, and no test of cache cold-start behavior.

Step 3 and Step 4 establish GDPR/CCPA data residency requirements for US and EU regions, but zero steps in the plan implement data residency enforcement at the query or storage layer. No geo-fencing of Iceberg table locations, no query routing by tenant region, no enforcement mechanism. This is a GDPR hard requirement that will block EU customers.

Step 17 AI feature acceptance criterion ('Natural language to SQL converts at least 80% of test queries') is unverifiable without a defined, adversarial test set. 80% on a hand-picked test set routinely drops to 40-50% on real user queries. No benchmark dataset (Spider, BIRD, WikiSQL) is referenced, and no evaluation methodology is defined.

Step 22 targets 80% line coverage but for a distributed data system with complex failure modes, line coverage is a poor proxy. No property-based testing, no chaos/fault injection tests, no data correctness tests that verify ETL output against source checksums. A passing test suite here would give false confidence.

Dependency order error: Step 15 (lineage) depends on Step 14 (catalog), but ETL execution lineage capture is part of Step 12 (ETL engine). Lineage events emitted by ETL need a store to write to before the catalog exists. The dependency graph forces lineage to be bolted on after ETL is built, guaranteeing a refactor of the ETL engine.

No API rate limiting or query resource governance at the API gateway layer. Step 13 specifies per-user memory/CPU quotas inside Trino, but nothing prevents a tenant from submitting 10,000 concurrent query submissions to the API layer, exhausting the query queue and starving other tenants before Trino-level governance activates.

No backup and restore implementation step exists. Step 28 defines DR procedures with RTO 4h / RPO 1h, but no step actually implements automated backups of PostgreSQL metadata, Elasticsearch indices, Neo4j graph data, or Iceberg table snapshots. DR procedures without tested restore tooling are documentation theater.
suggestions: Add an explicit architectural decision step (before Step 8) that resolves: Iceberg catalog backend, multi-tenant isolation model at each layer (Trino, Elasticsearch, Redis, PostgreSQL RLS), and data residency enforcement mechanism. These decisions block multiple downstream steps and their absence will cause rework.

Decompose Step 16 (BI connectors) into at minimum three steps: (1) Arrow Flight SQL server + SQLAlchemy dialect, (2) JDBC/ODBC via existing wire protocol compatibility, (3) native connectors for Tableau/Power BI. Descope Looker LookML export from MVP — it requires deep semantic layer integration that is disproportionately complex.

Replace Step 12's UDF sandbox acceptance criterion with a concrete design decision: select gVisor or Firecracker as the execution environment, define the resource limits (memory, CPU, wall-clock timeout), specify the syscall allowlist, and add a security review gate before merging UDF execution code.

For Step 15 column-level lineage, consider adopting OpenLineage as the event standard rather than building a custom ANTLR parser. This gives you integrations with dbt, Spark, Airflow out of the box and reduces the scope of the custom parser to SQL typed directly in the query editor only.

Add a cost metering and billing step. The ROI analysis in Step 2 positions this against Snowflake/BigQuery, both of which have consumption-based pricing. Without a metering system, you cannot bill customers or surface cost attribution per team/pipeline — a must-have for enterprise buyers.

Separate the load test in Step 26 into two distinct scenarios: (1) sustained load at expected p50 concurrency, (2) burst load at 10x p50. Also add a cold-start query scenario (no cached Iceberg metadata, no result cache) to validate the worst-case p95 the SLA must cover.

Step 9's Docker Compose will become unmaintainable with 8 services plus PostgreSQL, Elasticsearch, Neo4j, Redis, Vault, and Kafka. Consider splitting the local dev environment into a 'core' profile (API, auth, catalog) and a 'full' profile, with documented expectations for RAM requirements per profile.

Add tenant provisioning and offboarding to the RBAC step (Step 11). Offboarding requires cascading deletion of: Elasticsearch index, Neo4j subgraph, Iceberg namespace, PostgreSQL RLS policies, and audit log archival. Missing this creates GDPR right-to-erasure compliance gaps.

The observability stack (Step 25) should be deployed before system tests (Step 26), which it is — but it should also be instrumented before any backend service is considered 'done'. Add observability instrumentation as an acceptance criterion on Steps 11-16 rather than treating it as a post-integration concern.
missing: Iceberg catalog backend selection and justification (AWS Glue vs. Iceberg REST Catalog vs. Nessie) — affects Steps 8, 10, 12, 13, 14
Data residency enforcement mechanism for EU/US tenant separation at storage and query layers (required for GDPR Article 46)
Backup and restore implementation for all persistent stores (PostgreSQL, Elasticsearch, Neo4j, Iceberg snapshots) — the DR procedure in Step 28 has nothing to restore from
Cost metering and usage attribution system — without this, enterprise billing is impossible and the Snowflake/BigQuery comparison in the ROI analysis is not actionable
Schema registry for Kafka sources (Step 12 lists Kafka as a source but does not address schema evolution, Avro/Protobuf deserialization, or consumer group management)

Change data capture (CDC) connector design — ETL sources include PostgreSQL and MySQL but CDC (Debezium-style) is not addressed; polling-based ETL for OLTP sources at scale creates unacceptable source load
Tenant provisioning and offboarding workflow with cascading data deletion (GDPR right-to-erasure compliance)
API rate limiting and abuse prevention layer at the gateway (separate from Trino-level resource governance)
Per-tenant encryption key management (envelope encryption with tenant-specific KMS keys) — mentioned as a risk in Step 5 but never implemented
Data migration and import tooling for customers moving from Snowflake, BigQuery, or Redshift — this is a sales-cycle blocker and not in scope anywhere
Chaos engineering / fault injection test plan for distributed failure modes (Trino coordinator failure, Elasticsearch shard unavailability, Vault unreachability)
security_risks: Python UDF execution without a named, audited sandbox technology is a remote code execution vulnerability. If the sandbox is implemented incorrectly, a malicious UDF can read other tenants' Iceberg data, exfiltrate credentials from the Vault agent sidecar, or pivot to the Kubernetes control plane. This is the highest severity risk in the plan.

Shared Redis query result cache with tenant-namespaced keys relies on correct key construction in application code. A single bug in cache key generation leaks one tenant's query results to another. The acceptance criterion ('concurrent queries from different tenants do not share result cache entries') needs adversarial testing, not just a happy-path check.

ANTLR SQL parser (Step 15) can be DoS'd with adversarial SQL inputs — deeply nested CTEs, recursive queries, or pathologically complex join trees that trigger exponential parse time. No input validation or parse timeout is specified.

BI connector credentials (per-tool service accounts) are long-lived secrets that have read access to warehouse data. If a Tableau or Power BI service account credential is leaked, it can exfiltrate bulk data without triggering per-user quotas. Vault dynamic secrets rotation is listed but credential scope (read-all vs. read-specific-schema) is not defined.

Elasticsearch cluster is frequently misconfigured to be reachable from unintended networks. The plan puts it in private subnets but Step 14's schema inference from Glue Catalog sync could expose metadata to the Elasticsearch cluster from a broader network boundary. No Elasticsearch authentication model (API key vs. SAML) is specified.

OAuth 2.0 client credentials for service-to-service calls (in addition to mTLS) creates dual authentication paths. If the OAuth path is not disabled in production and mTLS is the intended enforcement mechanism, an attacker who compromises a client_secret bypasses mTLS entirely. The plan does not specify whether mTLS replaces or supplements OAuth for internal traffic.

Iceberg REST Catalog (if selected) exposes a metadata API that reveals table schemas and partition statistics to anyone with catalog-level access. Column-level RBAC at the query layer does not prevent schema enumeration at the catalog layer — a low-privilege user can infer sensitive data existence from column names and statistics without querying the data.
final_review.summary: This is an architecturally ambitious and well-structured plan that correctly identifies the six core domains of a cloud data warehouse and sequences most dependencies logically. The compliance coverage (ISO 27001, SOC 2, GDPR) is thorough, the API design is sound, and the observability/CI-CD choices are production-grade. However, the plan contains several critically underscoped components that will cause significant rework: the Python UDF sandbox is a named RCE risk with no implementation, column-level SQL lineage is treated as a straightforward parsing problem when it is a known hard problem in the industry, and the BI connector step conflates five distinct certification-level integrations into a single sprint-sized task. More structurally, three foundational architectural decisions are missing entirely — the Iceberg catalog backend, the multi-tenant isolation model per data store, and the data residency enforcement mechanism — and these decisions unblock or invalidate work in at least 10 downstream steps. The plan is not ready to execute as written; it needs the missing architectural decisions resolved and the three high-risk components (UDF sandbox, lineage parser, BI drivers) broken out into their own scoped sub-plans before any code is committed. Score reflects a well-intentioned comprehensive plan with fundamental execution risks that make the 54 generous — the UDF sandboxing and lineage gaps alone could each derail the product.

flaws: Step 12 specifies 'Python UDF sandbox prevents filesystem and network access' with zero implementation detail. No sandboxing technology is named (gVisor, Firecracker, seccomp profiles, nsjail). This is the highest-severity RCE vector in the entire plan and is treated as a one-line acceptance criterion. Production UDF sandboxing is a multi-sprint effort in itself.

Step 15 column-level lineage from ANTLR SQL parsing is massively underscoped. The acceptance criterion of '50 representative SQL patterns including JOINs, CTEs, and window functions' is a toy benchmark. Commercial lineage tools (Alation, Atlan, OpenMetadata) with years of engineering still fail on dynamic SQL, stored procedures, MERGE statements, lateral joins, and dialect variations. This will be the #1 source of post-launch bugs.

Step 16 builds JDBC/ODBC drivers and native connectors for Tableau, Power BI, Looker, Metabase, and Superset as a single plan step. Each of these is a substantial engineering project: Tableau has its own connector SDK and certification process, Power BI DirectQuery requires specific query folding semantics, Looker LookML export requires understanding the LookML schema model. This is 6-12 months of work presented as one step.

Step 13 performance SLA ('SELECT query on 1B row Iceberg table returns first page within 5 seconds') is an unqualified promise. It doesn't specify cluster size, query shape, partition pruning, whether data is cached, cold vs warm path, or network topology. This will be used in sales materials and will fail for non-trivial queries.

Multi-tenant isolation is mentioned throughout but never architecturally resolved. How does Trino enforce tenant isolation at query runtime? Separate catalogs per tenant? Shared catalog with row-filter policies? What happens to the query result cache in Redis — are keys namespaced per tenant? Elasticsearch: index-per-tenant or document-level security? These gaps are where cross-tenant leakage actually happens.

Step 8 specifies 7+ data stores: PostgreSQL, Elasticsearch, Neo4j/Neptune, TimescaleDB, Redis, S3, and the query engine's own storage. No single step addresses the operational burden of running all of these. The Docker Compose in Step 9 will be enormous and slow. The on-call burden is proportional to the number of distinct stores.

The Iceberg catalog backend is never decided. The plan uses Iceberg throughout (Steps 10, 13, 14) but never selects a metastore: AWS Glue, Hive Metastore, Iceberg REST Catalog, or Project Nessie. This choice affects the query engine, ETL engine, and catalog service at their foundation. It's a day-one architectural decision that's missing.

Step 9 specifies HashiCorp Vault as a hard startup dependency for all services with no Vault HA topology, no agent sidecar pattern, and no documented fallback. If Vault is unavailable at startup, every service fails to boot. This creates a single point of failure that invalidates the 99.9% availability SLO from Step 25.

Step 26 load test profile (100 concurrent query users, 10 ETL pipelines, 30 minutes) is inadequate for a product positioning against Snowflake and BigQuery. It proves nothing about production headroom. No mention of data volume at test time, no ramp-up simulation, no spike scenarios, and no test of cache cold-start behavior.

Step 3 and Step 4 establish GDPR/CCPA data residency requirements for US and EU regions, but zero steps in the plan implement data residency enforcement at the query or storage layer. No geo-fencing of Iceberg table locations, no query routing by tenant region, no enforcement mechanism. This is a GDPR hard requirement that will block EU customers.

Step 17 AI feature acceptance criterion ('Natural language to SQL converts at least 80% of test queries') is unverifiable without a defined, adversarial test set. 80% on a hand-picked test set routinely drops to 40-50% on real user queries. No benchmark dataset (Spider, BIRD, WikiSQL) is referenced, and no evaluation methodology is defined.

Step 22 targets 80% line coverage but for a distributed data system with complex failure modes, line coverage is a poor proxy. No property-based testing, no chaos/fault injection tests, no data correctness tests that verify ETL output against source checksums. A passing test suite here would give false confidence.

Dependency order error: Step 15 (lineage) depends on Step 14 (catalog), but ETL execution lineage capture is part of Step 12 (ETL engine). Lineage events emitted by ETL need a store to write to before the catalog exists. The dependency graph forces lineage to be bolted on after ETL is built, guaranteeing a refactor of the ETL engine.

No API rate limiting or query resource governance at the API gateway layer. Step 13 specifies per-user memory/CPU quotas inside Trino, but nothing prevents a tenant from submitting 10,000 concurrent query submissions to the API layer, exhausting the query queue and starving other tenants before Trino-level governance activates.

No backup and restore implementation step exists. Step 28 defines DR procedures with RTO 4h / RPO 1h, but no step actually implements automated backups of PostgreSQL metadata, Elasticsearch indices, Neo4j graph data, or Iceberg table snapshots. DR procedures without tested restore tooling are documentation theater.
suggestions: Add an explicit architectural decision step (before Step 8) that resolves: Iceberg catalog backend, multi-tenant isolation model at each layer (Trino, Elasticsearch, Redis, PostgreSQL RLS), and data residency enforcement mechanism. These decisions block multiple downstream steps and their absence will cause rework.

Decompose Step 16 (BI connectors) into at minimum three steps: (1) Arrow Flight SQL server + SQLAlchemy dialect, (2) JDBC/ODBC via existing wire protocol compatibility, (3) native connectors for Tableau/Power BI. Descope Looker LookML export from MVP — it requires deep semantic layer integration that is disproportionately complex.

Replace Step 12's UDF sandbox acceptance criterion with a concrete design decision: select gVisor or Firecracker as the execution environment, define the resource limits (memory, CPU, wall-clock timeout), specify the syscall allowlist, and add a security review gate before merging UDF execution code.

For Step 15 column-level lineage, consider adopting OpenLineage as the event standard rather than building a custom ANTLR parser. This gives you integrations with dbt, Spark, Airflow out of the box and reduces the scope of the custom parser to SQL typed directly in the query editor only.

Add a cost metering and billing step. The ROI analysis in Step 2 positions this against Snowflake/BigQuery, both of which have consumption-based pricing. Without a metering system, you cannot bill customers or surface cost attribution per team/pipeline — a must-have for enterprise buyers.

Separate the load test in Step 26 into two distinct scenarios: (1) sustained load at expected p50 concurrency, (2) burst load at 10x p50. Also add a cold-start query scenario (no cached Iceberg metadata, no result cache) to validate the worst-case p95 the SLA must cover.

Step 9's Docker Compose will become unmaintainable with 8 services plus PostgreSQL, Elasticsearch, Neo4j, Redis, Vault, and Kafka. Consider splitting the local dev environment into a 'core' profile (API, auth, catalog) and a 'full' profile, with documented expectations for RAM requirements per profile.

Add tenant provisioning and offboarding to the RBAC step (Step 11). Offboarding requires cascading deletion of: Elasticsearch index, Neo4j subgraph, Iceberg namespace, PostgreSQL RLS policies, and audit log archival. Missing this creates GDPR right-to-erasure compliance gaps.

The observability stack (Step 25) should be deployed before system tests (Step 26), which it is — but it should also be instrumented before any backend service is considered 'done'. Add observability instrumentation as an acceptance criterion on Steps 11-16 rather than treating it as a post-integration concern.
missing: Iceberg catalog backend selection and justification (AWS Glue vs. Iceberg REST Catalog vs. Nessie) — affects Steps 8, 10, 12, 13, 14
Data residency enforcement mechanism for EU/US tenant separation at storage and query layers (required for GDPR Article 46)
Backup and restore implementation for all persistent stores (PostgreSQL, Elasticsearch, Neo4j, Iceberg snapshots) — the DR procedure in Step 28 has nothing to restore from
Cost metering and usage attribution system — without this, enterprise billing is impossible and the Snowflake/BigQuery comparison in the ROI analysis is not actionable
Schema registry for Kafka sources (Step 12 lists Kafka as a source but does not address schema evolution, Avro/Protobuf deserialization, or consumer group management)

Change data capture (CDC) connector design — ETL sources include PostgreSQL and MySQL but CDC (Debezium-style) is not addressed; polling-based ETL for OLTP sources at scale creates unacceptable source load
Tenant provisioning and offboarding workflow with cascading data deletion (GDPR right-to-erasure compliance)
API rate limiting and abuse prevention layer at the gateway (separate from Trino-level resource governance)
Per-tenant encryption key management (envelope encryption with tenant-specific KMS keys) — mentioned as a risk in Step 5 but never implemented
Data migration and import tooling for customers moving from Snowflake, BigQuery, or Redshift — this is a sales-cycle blocker and not in scope anywhere
Chaos engineering / fault injection test plan for distributed failure modes (Trino coordinator failure, Elasticsearch shard unavailability, Vault unreachability)
security_risks: Python UDF execution without a named, audited sandbox technology is a remote code execution vulnerability. If the sandbox is implemented incorrectly, a malicious UDF can read other tenants' Iceberg data, exfiltrate credentials from the Vault agent sidecar, or pivot to the Kubernetes control plane. This is the highest severity risk in the plan.

Shared Redis query result cache with tenant-namespaced keys relies on correct key construction in application code. A single bug in cache key generation leaks one tenant's query results to another. The acceptance criterion ('concurrent queries from different tenants do not share result cache entries') needs adversarial testing, not just a happy-path check.

ANTLR SQL parser (Step 15) can be DoS'd with adversarial SQL inputs — deeply nested CTEs, recursive queries, or pathologically complex join trees that trigger exponential parse time. No input validation or parse timeout is specified.

BI connector credentials (per-tool service accounts) are long-lived secrets that have read access to warehouse data. If a Tableau or Power BI service account credential is leaked, it can exfiltrate bulk data without triggering per-user quotas. Vault dynamic secrets rotation is listed but credential scope (read-all vs. read-specific-schema) is not defined.

Elasticsearch cluster is frequently misconfigured to be reachable from unintended networks. The plan puts it in private subnets but Step 14's schema inference from Glue Catalog sync could expose metadata to the Elasticsearch cluster from a broader network boundary. No Elasticsearch authentication model (API key vs. SAML) is specified.

OAuth 2.0 client credentials for service-to-service calls (in addition to mTLS) creates dual authentication paths. If the OAuth path is not disabled in production and mTLS is the intended enforcement mechanism, an attacker who compromises a client_secret bypasses mTLS entirely. The plan does not specify whether mTLS replaces or supplements OAuth for internal traffic.

Iceberg REST Catalog (if selected) exposes a metadata API that reveals table schemas and partition statistics to anyone with catalog-level access. Column-level RBAC at the query layer does not prevent schema enumeration at the catalog layer — a low-privilege user can infer sensitive data existence from column names and statistics without querying the data.
history.summary: This is an architecturally ambitious and well-structured plan that correctly identifies the six core domains of a cloud data warehouse and sequences most dependencies logically. The compliance coverage (ISO 27001, SOC 2, GDPR) is thorough, the API design is sound, and the observability/CI-CD choices are production-grade. However, the plan contains several critically underscoped components that will cause significant rework: the Python UDF sandbox is a named RCE risk with no implementation, column-level SQL lineage is treated as a straightforward parsing problem when it is a known hard problem in the industry, and the BI connector step conflates five distinct certification-level integrations into a single sprint-sized task. More structurally, three foundational architectural decisions are missing entirely — the Iceberg catalog backend, the multi-tenant isolation model per data store, and the data residency enforcement mechanism — and these decisions unblock or invalidate work in at least 10 downstream steps. The plan is not ready to execute as written; it needs the missing architectural decisions resolved and the three high-risk components (UDF sandbox, lineage parser, BI drivers) broken out into their own scoped sub-plans before any code is committed. Score reflects a well-intentioned comprehensive plan with fundamental execution risks that make the 54 generous — the UDF sandboxing and lineage gaps alone could each derail the product.
